objectives.text: Advise on ICT procurement standards such as EN 301 549
Analyze design elements (e.g., headings, landmarks, color contrast) to determine their impact on diverse user groups' navigation and comprehension strategies.
Analyze how consistent web design supports users with cognitive disabilities
Analyze how design decisions impact accessibility for people with various disabilities
Analyze how DOM content affects information available in the Accessibility Tree
Analyze how focus order, event handling (onClick, onKeyDown, etc.), and ARIA states can either improve or harm the user experience for assistive technology.
Analyze how JavaScript can improve or damage accessibility depending on its use
Analyze how the accessibility tree is exposed to assistive technology and **verify** ARIA roles, states, and properties in different screen reader/browser combinations.
Analyze the role of ARIA in enhancing assistive technology support for dynamic content
Apply accessible procurement maturity models (PDAA, W3C maturity model)
Apply ACT Rules in testing methodologies
Apply appropriate ARIA roles to non-semantic elements to convey meaning
Apply appropriate techniques to provide accessible names and descriptions to elements
Apply aria-activedescendant property for managing focus within composite widgets
Apply authoring practices for custom widgets including semantic structure and keyboard behavior
Apply conventions for keyboard interaction within ARIA widgets
Apply EN 301 549 Annex B to determine impact on different user groups
Apply hybrid approaches (immediate remediation for critical issues, long-term rewrite planning)
Apply keyboard conventions (tab, arrow keys, enter, space) appropriately for custom widgets
Apply keyboard interaction conventions from the APG to custom widgets
Apply proper ARIA roles, states, and properties when implementing custom controls
Apply roving tabindex technique for managing focus within composite widgets
Apply standard keystrokes for interaction with interactive elements in native HTML
Apply standard keystrokes for native HTML interactive elements
Apply techniques to notify screen readers of new content (e.g., focus management, aria-live, page title updates)
Apply the 5 rules for using ARIA when creating custom widgets
Apply the five rules for using ARIA correctly
Apply the Plan-Create-Test cycle for accessibility integration
Apply the WCAG-EM methodology's five main steps for website evaluation
Apply the WCAG-EM methodology to define scope, explore site content, select a representative sample, and evaluate conformance thoroughly.
Apply user-centered design principles that consider diverse abilities and assistive technology users
Apply W3C-recommended techniques (e.g., sufficient techniques, failure techniques) to specific WCAG success criteria in real coding scenarios.
Assess code complexity and maintainability when deciding on approach
Assess consequences of different types of accessibility flaws
Assess cost-benefit of remediation efforts
Assess feasibility of particular solutions in different contexts
Assess mobile interaction (touch gestures, orientation changes) to **ensure** elements remain accessible and do not require two-dimensional scrolling.
Assess the compatibility of a chosen technology stack (front-end framework, JS library) with major screen reader/browser pairs and **recommend** fallback or polyfill strategies.
Assess whether alternative input methods (switch control, voice input) can navigate and operate interactive elements effectively.
Audit existing systems as part of procurement process
Categorize accessibility issues based on severity (blockers vs. minor nuisances) and **formulate** a remediation timeline that balances user impact and resource constraints.
Categorize ARIA roles (Abstract, Widget, Document Structure, Landmark, Live Region, Window)
Collaborate with design and development teams to **implement** new UI patterns or code libraries that systematically eliminate repeated accessibility faults.
Combine automated and manual testing approaches effectively
Communicate purpose, approach, and strategy for remediation clearly
Compare and contrast the similarities and differences between WCAG and EN 301 549
Compare keyboard-only navigation to screen reader navigation
Compare native HTML controls' built-in accessibility features with custom (ARIA-based) components, highlighting pros and cons.
Compare screen reader behavior and support across different platforms and devices
Compare screen reader modes (browse/read mode, forms mode, application mode)
Compare the accessibility implications of applying onClick() to native semantic elements versus non-semantic elements
Compare the built-in accessibility features of native HTML elements to custom implementations
Compare the scope of WCAG (2.0, 2.1, 2.2), ATAG 2.0, and EN 301 549, and **identify** how each set of requirements influences web accessibility design.
Compare the strengths and limitations of automated tools, browser-based linters, spider tools, and bookmarklets for accessibility testing.
Conduct accessibility testing using multiple assistive technologies and methods
Conduct product testing during procurement
Conduct screen reader testing to confirm that name, role, value, and state changes of interactive elements are conveyed properly.
Conduct task-based usability tests with native assistive technology users
Configure ARIA live regions correctly so content is reliably announced across platforms
Confirm that instructions are not based on sensory characteristics alone
Consider accessibility performance during contract negotiations and renewals
Consider available resources (time, budget, expertise) in approach selection
Consider impact on interface when prioritizing remediation
Construct a custom widget (e.g., a modal dialog or accordion) using the WAI-ARIA Authoring Practices, ensuring correct roles, states, and properties.
Coordinate with product owners, designers, and developers to incorporate accessibility checks into acceptance criteria and "definition of done."
Create Accessibility Conformance Reports (ACRs)
Define "accessibility-supported" technology according to WCAG conformance requirements
Define accessible name and accessible description and explain how they differ
Define and distinguish between "assistive technology" and "adaptive strategies"
Demonstrate correct focus management using `aria-activedescendant` or roving `tabindex` in interactive widgets.
Demonstrate correct keyboard interaction models (e.g., tab vs. arrow keys) for composite components such as menus, tabs, and disclosure widgets.
Demonstrate how JavaScript event handlers can be made device-independent (e.g., supporting both pointer and keyboard input).
Demonstrate how to communicate high-impact barriers (e.g., no keyboard support, missing alt text on key images) to stakeholders and developers.
Demonstrate how to manage announcements of dynamic page updates (e.g., with `aria-live`) when navigation or content changes do not trigger a full page reload.
Demonstrate how to repurpose a non-semantic element into a fully accessible custom button or link using proper ARIA attributes, keyboard events, and visible focus.
Demonstrate how to use color-contrast analyzers, focus inspectors, or code validators to confirm partial compliance with WCAG criteria.
Demonstrate manual keyboard testing to detect missing focus indicators, incorrect tab order, or keyboard traps.
Demonstrate usage of at least two popular screen reader/browser combinations (e.g., NVDA + Firefox, VoiceOver + Safari) to confirm consistent user experience.
Demonstrate when and how to send focus to new content using JavaScript
Describe assistive technologies used by people with reading difficulties (text-to-speech, reading software)
Describe challenges faced by magnification users (orientation, focus tracking)
Describe common navigation strategies used by screen reader users (e.g., headings, landmarks, element lists)
Describe each team member's role in delivering accessible products
Describe how screen readers use the Accessibility API to retrieve information
Describe how the Accessibility API provides information to assistive technologies
Describe how users with cognitive disabilities may use assistive technologies (screen readers, magnification, focus tools)
Describe how voice recognition commands are often based on keyboard operations
Describe input methods used by people with motor disabilities (keyboard, switches, voice, eye-tracking)
Describe key strategies that users with blindness, low vision, motor disabilities, auditory disabilities, and cognitive/learning disabilities employ to interact with web content.
Describe strategies used by people with low vision (text enlargement, zoom, color changes, magnification)
Describe the additional requirements in EN 301 549 beyond WCAG Success Criteria (38 additional requirements)
Describe the diverse range of cognitive disabilities and their impact on web use
Describe the impact of device-independent event handlers on accessibility
Describe the importance of visible focus indicators for keyboard users
Describe the limitations in WCAG (e.g., certain gaps for cognitive or motion-based issues) and **propose** best-practice enhancements.
Describe the meaning and intent of the two main sections of ATAG 2.0 (Part A: Accessible user interface; Part B: Supporting accessible content production)
Describe the MouseGrid technique for users who cannot identify specific clickable elements
Describe the risks associated with using polyfills for accessibility
Describe the three types of techniques (Sufficient, Failure, Advisory) and the W3C vetting process for techniques
Describe the WAI-ARIA 1.2 model of roles, states, and properties
Determine budget and resources for accessibility implementation
Determine if issues have high impact that prevents task completion (CAPTCHA without alternative, keyboard-inaccessible controls, hidden content)
Determine when an inaccessible outcome results from design flaws versus poor technology support
Determine when to use WAI-ARIA 1.2 versus standard HTML elements
Determine when users need to be notified about passively loaded AJAX content
Develop a procurement checklist incorporating EN 301 549 or WCAG references to ensure new products and services meet accessibility requirements.
Develop suitable questions for tender documents and RFPs
Differentiate between Agile and Waterfall project management methodologies regarding accessibility quality assurance
Differentiate between fixing particular issues and complete page redesign
Differentiate between normative and non-normative information in ATAG 2.0
Differentiate between normative and non-normative information in WCAG 2.2
Differentiate between normative and non-normative parts of WCAG, WAI-ARIA, and ATAG, and **justify** why that distinction matters for conformance.
Distinguish between accessibility and user experience design disciplines
Distinguish between accessibility issues found by automated tools versus those requiring manual testing
Distinguish between ARIA states and properties and explain when values typically change
Distinguish between automated accessibility practices in authoring tools and practices requiring author/user input
Distinguish between critical issues and medium-impact issues
Distinguish between design usability, accessibility, and conformance to specifications
Distinguish between ideal/best solution and "good enough" solution based on project constraints
Distinguish between normative and non-normative content in WCAG documentation
Distinguish between "quick fixes" that resolve localized errors and "redesign" approaches that address deeper structural problems.
Distinguish between screen reader bugs and actual accessibility issues
Distinguish between semantic HTML controls (e.g., `<button>`, `<a>`) and generic elements (e.g., `<div>`) in terms of built-in accessibility.
Distinguish between the "Patterns" and "Practices" sections of the APG
Distinguish between the three conformance levels (A, AA, AAA) and identify the conformance level of each WCAG 2.2 success criterion
Distinguish between true accessibility failures and poor design practices not covered in specifications
Distinguish between VPAT formats (2.4 Rev 508, 2.4 Rev EU, 2.4 Rev WCAG, 2.4 INT)
Distinguish between WCAG conformance failures and optional best practices
Document accessibility goals and define conformance in "Definition of done"
Document accessibility issues and provide remediation recommendations
Engage appropriate stakeholders in implementation recommendations
Ensure accessibility is systematically considered in purchasing decisions
Ensure team members have access to accessibility expertise and training
Establish a continuous testing process (automated + manual) that catches regressions after updates or new releases.
Evaluate a website's accessibility across multiple devices (desktop, tablet, mobile) and assistive technologies.
Evaluate existing custom controls for missing or incorrect ARIA markup, and **recommend** solutions to align them with best practices.
Evaluate legal risk of identified issues
Evaluate page titles for descriptive topic or purpose
Evaluate quality of vendor-provided ACRs
Evaluate severity and scope of defects to determine remediation vs. rewrite
Evaluate tender responses, ACRs, and VPATs
Evaluate that dynamic status messages are conveyed using live regions
Evaluate that focus order is logical, intuitive, and predictable
Evaluate the importance and urgency of dynamically loaded content to determine notification strategy
Evaluate the power and limitations of automated accessibility authoring features
Evaluate the user experience of a single-page application with typical assistive technologies and **propose** solutions for any discovered gaps.
Evaluate vendor Accessibility Conformance Reports (VPAT/ACR) to determine product compatibility with organizational accessibility standards.
Evaluate websites and applications for conformance to WCAG 2.2 Level AA
Evaluate when a custom role overrides native semantics and understand the implications
Evaluate when it's appropriate to implement workarounds for browser or assistive technology bugs
Evaluate whether semantic markup conveys meaning appropriately (headings, lists, tables, paragraphs)
Explain accessibility strategies for users with cognitive disabilities
Explain how accessibility can be integrated into agile vs. waterfall lifecycles, focusing on early detection of issues.
Explain how ACT Rules can guide consistent test procedures across multiple tools and testers.
Explain how ARIA attributes are mapped to the operating system's accessibility API
Explain how ATAG 2.0 applies to web content authoring tools
Explain how content can be accessible but not usable for end-users
Explain how keyboard support serves as a requirement for multiple types of assistive technologies
Explain how magnification software works and its capabilities
Explain how screen readers function and convert content to speech or braille
Explain how semantic HTML elements have implicit ARIA semantics
Explain how speech recognition software works for navigation and interaction
Explain how text-to-speech software differs from full screen readers
Explain how the Document Object Model (DOM) relates to the Accessibility Tree
Explain how the interaction model differs for blind touchscreen users versus sighted touchscreen users
Explain how touch interaction changes with a screen reader turned on (e.g., iOS VoiceOver gestures) and **demonstrate** design patterns to support these gestures.
Explain how touch interactions change when screen readers are active (e.g., gesture-based navigation on mobile)
Explain how users with low vision often combine magnification with screen reading
Explain how WCAG is represented in EN 301 549
Explain keyboard navigation strategies and why sequential navigation can be tedious
Explain progressive enhancement and **outline** the implications of turning JavaScript off or using older browsers for accessibility.
Explain the Accessible Name and Description Computation algorithm
Explain the additive approach of WCAG versions and how WCAG 2.2 conforms to earlier versions
Explain the advantages of using standard HTML controls over custom controls
Explain the benefits of designing with accessibility in mind versus remediation
Explain the benefits of providing content in multiple formats (text, images, audio)
Explain the concept of conformance levels (A, AA, AAA) and **defend** the rationale for choosing a specific level in a project or legal context.
Explain the concept of progressive enhancement and its importance for accessibility
Explain the difference between WCAG 2.0, 2.1, and 2.2 Success Criteria
Explain the five primary rules of ARIA usage (e.g., "use native HTML first," "do not change native semantics unless necessary").
Explain the importance of coding to standards rather than to quirks of specific technologies
Explain the importance of large click targets and clear visual indicators for pointer users
Explain the importance of semantic markup and structured content for this user group
Explain the importance of visible focus indicators for users with low vision
Explain the intent and scope of EN 301 549 requirements that extend beyond WCAG
Explain the intent, requirements, and impact of each WCAG 2.2 principle, guideline, and success criterion
Explain the pass/fail conformance model and its implications for accessibility testing
Explain the purpose and impact of WAI-ARIA 1.2 on web accessibility
Explain the purpose and limitations of ACT Rules
Explain the purpose of ACRs and VPATs
Explain the role of end users in assessing and validating remediation approaches
Explain the structure of WCAG 2.2 by identifying the four principles (POUR), guidelines, and success criteria levels (A, AA, AAA).
Explain the value of user testing with people with various disabilities
Explain user expectations regarding focus movement in web applications
Explain when to use role="application" and why it should be used sparingly
Explain why certain ARIA roles or HTML5 elements might not be fully supported across older browsers, and how to mitigate these gaps.
Explain why focus order must be logical and intuitive for screen reader users
Explain why new technologies may not be immediately accessibility-supported
Focus remediation on key content, high-traffic pages, and essential tasks
Foster long-term, sustainable accessibility improvements across supply chain
Identify accessibility challenges unique to single-page applications (SPAs)
Identify accessibility needs associated with different types of disabilities
Identify accessibility techniques not yet documented in WCAG
Identify and apply Sufficient Techniques, Failure Techniques, and Advisory Techniques for each success criterion
Identify and explain the four POUR principles (Perceivable, Operable, Understandable, Robust) and their relationship to guidelines and success criteria
Identify built-in and third-party assistive technologies for users with motor disabilities (Dragon, switch controls, eye-tracking)
Identify built-in magnification features across different platforms
Identify common pitfalls in dynamic content updates (e.g., partial page refreshes with AJAX) and **implement** focus management or live regions to keep screen reader users informed.
Identify common user needs for auditory disabilities (e.g., accurate captions, transcripts) and evaluate if they are met.
Identify CSS issues affecting assistive technology users
Identify font and text presentation best practices for readability
Identify groups of users with reading difficulties (ADHD, dyslexia, Irlen syndrome, memory loss)
Identify how accessibility integrates into the entire product life cycle (concept, requirements, design, prototyping, development, QA, user testing, support, regression testing)
Identify how assistive technologies (e.g., screen readers, magnifiers, voice input) interact with browsers and what "accessibility-supported" means under WCAG.
Identify how content reading order follows DOM order in screen readers
Identify issues for methods, technologies, and strategies used by people with disabilities
Identify low-effort repairs that can be addressed quickly
Identify methods to notify screen readers that new content has been added dynamically (e.g., ARIA live regions, focus management)
Identify most common screen readers and recommended browser combinations
Identify potential cognitive barriers (e.g., complex language or form instructions) and **recommend** simpler language or multi-step guidance.
Identify potential issues arising from specific browser–screen reader combinations (e.g., JAWS + Chrome vs. NVDA + Firefox).
Identify recommended screen reader and browser combinations
Identify requirements beyond WCAG that are relevant for compliance with the Web Accessibility Directive 2016/2102
Identify semantic structure requirements for ARIA roles, including required parent/child roles and required attributes
Identify situations when custom controls are necessary or appropriate
Identify sources that contribute to accessible names (e.g., aria-label, aria-labelledby, alt text, label elements)
Identify that ACT Rules check for failures but passing does not guarantee full conformance
Identify that automated tools cannot determine conformance alone
Identify the five types of information provided by ARIA attributes (role, state, name, description, relationship)
Identify the four Success Criteria required for non-interference conformance
Identify the importance of plain language, simple layout, and predictable design
Identify the limitations of the APG sample code (e.g., lack of comprehensive compatibility testing)
Identify the needs of users with different types of disabilities as outlined in EN 301 549's Functional Performance statements
Identify the range of motor disabilities and their impact on computer use
Identify the types of technologies addressed by EN 301 549
Identify when independent accessibility professionals should create ACRs
Identify when it's appropriate to implement workarounds, compromises, or polyfills for assistive technology bugs
Identify which Success Criteria apply to Level A, AA, and AAA conformance levels
Implement accessibility in information architecture and user experience
Implement accessible names and descriptions using `aria-label`, `aria-labelledby`, and `aria-describedby`, and **justify** which approach is most appropriate in a given scenario.
Implement alternative access plans for users when defects exist
Implement appropriate notification methods when AJAX content loads as a result of user action
Implement a vendor management process that includes accessibility roadmap reviews, defect remediation, and ongoing conformance monitoring.
Implement focus changes appropriately when new views or dialogs appear, ensuring screen-reader and keyboard-only users can track SPA updates.
Implement keyboard interaction models for ARIA custom widgets, including general patterns and widget-specific patterns
Implement keyboard interaction models for custom ARIA widgets
Implement proper focus management when JavaScript changes visual focus
Implement techniques for providing accessible names and descriptions to custom widgets
Include binding accessibility commitments in contracts
Incorporate accessibility into procurement process from the beginning
Integrate knowledge from multiple domains to create comprehensive accessible web solutions
Interpret ACR conformance levels (Supports, Partially Supports, Does Not Support, Not Applicable)
Interpret automated testing results and identify limitations
Interpret automated test results to confirm valid failures, eliminate false positives, and plan follow-up manual checks.
Interpret results of automatic testing and identify false positives and omissions
Interpret the self-scoping (applicability) language used in EN 301 549
Involve people with disabilities in requirements and early development stages
Justify which issues must be addressed first by assessing legal risk, user impact, and overall usability.
List browser and operating system settings that support users with reading difficulties
List different types of assistive technologies and adaptive strategies used by people with disabilities
List the five WCAG conformance requirements that content must meet to achieve conformance
List the most commonly used screen readers (JAWS, NVDA, VoiceOver)
List types of software tools available (site-wide scanning, server-based analysis, unit testing, integration testing, browser developer tools, browser add-ons, simulators, guided manual testing)
Manage vendors including defect remediation and accessibility roadmaps
Map accessibility issues to specific WCAG Success Criteria accurately
Map discovered accessibility barriers to the appropriate WCAG success criterion (or EN 301 549 clause) and **distinguish** conformance failures from best-practice gaps.
Measure progress over time using maturity model frameworks
Monitor vendor performance in defect management
Navigate and utilize the ARIA Authoring Practice Guide (APG) to implement accessible custom widgets
Navigate ARIA custom widgets using keyboard and screen readers
Navigate content via landmarks and headings using screen readers
Obtain roadmaps for addressing known accessibility issues from vendors
Perform usability walk-throughs with simulated low-vision settings (zoom, high contrast) to find layout or text-flow issues.
Plan for future-proofing through comprehensive rewrites when appropriate
Prioritize issues based on impact (high, medium, low) on users with disabilities
Prioritize repeated issues that occur across templates, component libraries, or design systems
Prioritize user experience in remediation decisions
Propose ARIA-based or native HTML solutions for each conformance failure, **explaining** why a given fix is both accessible and feasible.
Propose layout or structural revisions (e.g., skip links, consistent navigation) that accommodate multiple disability groups.
Provide guidance on good governance practices for procurement
Provide practical and simple hints for better web accessibility
Recognize diversity among users with disabilities affects user experience
Recognize limitations of personal assistive technology knowledge
Recognize that automated tools can include false positives
Recognize that multiple techniques can satisfy a Success Criterion
Recognize that WCAG conformance is minimum requirement, not comprehensive usability guarantee
Recognize the limitations of WCAG 2.2 (pseudomotion, color blindness, cognitive load, etc.)
Recommend contract language that mandates the vendor to fix accessibility defects within specified timeframes and supply updated VPATs.
Recommend standard HTML controls whenever possible and **justify** the business and user benefits of reducing custom code for accessibility.
Recommend supplemental guidance beyond WCAG requirements for better usability
Refer to WCAG Understanding texts, Techniques, and ARIA Authoring Practices Guidelines
Respond to tender questions regarding accessibility
Select appropriate testing tools for different stages of web development process
Specify recommended browser and screen reader combinations for Windows, macOS, iOS, and Android platforms
Suggest contract wording regarding accessibility
Summarize the rationale for using the latest WCAG 2.2 and **evaluate** the impact of new success criteria (e.g., Focus Not Obscured) on website design.
Test adequate spacing between touch targets
Test ARIA constructs and components with screen readers
Test for keyboard traps
Test for no loss of functionality when zooming to 200%
Test sufficient contrast (including text on gradients or images)
Test sufficient contrast of non-text elements (icons, input field borders)
Test that all functionality is operable via keyboard with no keyboard trap
Test that audio can be turned off for voice control users
Test that automatically playing audio can be turned off
Test that automatic captioning errors are corrected
Test that color alone is not used to convey information
Test that content appearing on hover/focus can be dismissed, doesn't auto-close, and can be hovered over
Test that content order presented to assistive technology is meaningful
Test that device motion functionality has alternative UI controls
Test that error messages are identifiable and described
Test that error suggestions are provided
Test that focus indicator is visible
Test that focus order is logical, intuitive, and predictable
Test that hidden content is appropriately hidden both visually and programmatically
Test that interactive elements use native controls or proper ARIA markup
Test that language changes are marked up accordingly
Test that methods exist to bypass repeated content
Test that motion activation can be disabled
Test that multiple ways to locate pages are available
Test that multipoint or path-based gestures can be operated with single pointer
Test that multipoint or path-based gestures have single pointer alternatives
Test that navigation mechanisms are positioned consistently
Test that navigation mechanisms occur in same order across pages
Test that no two-dimensional scrolling is necessary
Test that no UI component causes unexpected context change on focus
Test that page titles describe topic or purpose
Test that role, name, and value of interactive components are available and updated correctly
Test that scrolling or moving content can be stopped by the user
Test that text alternatives for images are accurate and meaningful
Test that text can be resized to 200%
Test that the correct page language is set for proper screen reader pronunciation
Test that titles, headings, and form labels are descriptive
Test that users can stop animation or it stops automatically after 5 seconds
Test web designs for accessibility across various platforms, browsers, and assistive technologies
Understand different screen reader modes (browse mode, forms mode, application mode, VoiceOver rotor, TalkBack menu)
Understand how ARIA live region properties affect the announcement of dynamic content to assistive technologies and determine their default behaviors.
Understand how ARIA live region properties and values impact assistive technology behavior and user experience.
Understand how focus order impacts accessibility and evaluate whether the focus sequence of interactive elements aligns with logical and intuitive navigation for all users.
Understand how focus order impacts usability and accessibility, and evaluate whether the focus sequence aligns with a logical, meaningful, and user-friendly navigation experience.
Understand the role and significance of international standards in promoting web accessibility and identify examples of such standards.
Update page titles dynamically using JavaScript (document.title) in SPAs
Use accessibility API viewers (Accessibility Viewer for Windows, XCode Accessibility Inspector for MacOS)
Use ACR information to guide product reviews
Use ARIA states and properties to provide information about element status to assistive technologies
Use at least one site-wide scanning tool and one browser-based developer tool (e.g., Axe, WAVE, Lighthouse) to **detect** markup or ARIA errors.
Use browser developer tools and add-ons for accessibility testing
Use browser's built-in Development Tools to inspect source code
Use guided manual testing tools based on heuristics (Accessibility Insights, ARC Platform, Axe Auditor, JAWS Inspect)
Use JavaScript to toggle ARIA state attributes to convey component state to assistive technologies
Use magnifier or high-contrast settings to **detect** any text or interface elements that become unusable or unreadable.
Use maturity models and tools to illustrate progress and sustainability
Use maturity models to assess current accessibility practices
Use screen reader forms mode and application mode appropriately
Use simulators for colorblindness and low vision testing (Colour Oracle, No Coffee vision simulator)
Use single-purpose tools (Headings Map, PEAT, Contrast Checker, Colour Contrast Analyser)
Verify all content/functionality available at desktop widths is accessible on small touch screens
Verify all functionality is operable via keyboard
Verify all functionality is operable via keyboard with no keyboard trap
Verify alternatives for video (captions, audio description) and audio are provided and accurate
Verify character key shortcuts can be turned off, remapped, or are only active on focus
Verify color is not used as sole means to convey information
Verify components with same functionality are identified consistently
Verify descriptive labels or instructions for user input elements are available
Verify device motion functionality has alternative UI controls
Verify error messages are programmatically identifiable and described in text
Verify error prevention mechanisms exist for legal and financial data
Verify focus movement within components/widgets is as expected
Verify headings and labels are descriptive
Verify headings, lists, and structural elements are marked up correctly
Verify input fields have appropriate autocomplete attributes
Verify large touch target sizes
Verify links are descriptive in context
Verify link text is descriptive or determinable with context
Verify methods exist to bypass repeated content (skip links, landmarks, section headings)
Verify methods to bypass repeated content exist
Verify motion activation can be disabled to prevent accidental activation
Verify no content flashes above threshold
Verify no loss of content/functionality when text spacing is changed
Verify no loss of content or functionality when users change text spacing
Verify no loss of functionality when zooming to 200%
Verify no restriction to single display orientation
Verify no restriction to single display orientation (portrait or landscape)
Verify no UI component causes unexpected context change when setting is changed
Verify programmatic labels match visual labels
Verify session timeouts can be turned off or adjusted
Verify state changes are announced to screen reader users
Verify sufficient contrast of text elements
Verify text can be resized to 200%
Verify that alternatives exist for images and video content
Verify that automatically playing audio can be turned off
Verify that dynamically updated or hidden elements are properly announced or hidden from screen readers.
Verify that focus indicator is easily visible
Verify that link text is descriptive or determinable with context
Verify that only up-events execute functions or users can reverse/undo
Verify transcripts are provided for audio-only content
Verify use of real text instead of images of text
Verify videos have accurate captions
Verify visual labels match programmatic labels

associations_by_canvas_id.question_preview: Which of the following could best be described as a multi-national standard?
associations_by_canvas_id.objective_text: Understand the role and significance of international standards in promoting web accessibility and identify examples of such standards.

associations_by_canvas_id.objective_text: Analyze design elements (e.g., headings, landmarks, color contrast) to determine their impact on diverse user groups' navigation and comprehension strategies.
